name: Priam
description: Priam, son of Laomedon, was the last king of Troy before its fall during the Trojan War. He reigned during a prosperous era, fathering a large family, notably Hector, Paris, Cassandra, and many others. Priam was known for his wisdom, piety, and just rule, earning the respect of his people and even some of his enemies. Despite his advanced age during the war, he exhibited courage and dignity, particularly in his desperate attempts to protect his city and family from the Achaean onslaught. His most poignant moment is his courageous journey into the Achaean camp to plead with Achilles for the return of Hector's body, demonstrating remarkable humility and paternal love. Priam's death at the hands of Achilles' son, Neoptolemus, on the altar of Zeus Polieus, marked the tragic end of the Trojan royal line and symbolized the utter destruction of Troy. His story serves as a powerful reminder of the futility of war and the enduring strength of human emotions, especially the love of a father for his son. He embodies the tragic hero, a noble figure brought down by fate and the actions of others. His reign and subsequent demise are central to understanding the Trojan War's themes of honor, loss, and the devastating consequences of conflict. Priam's legacy continues to resonate, representing the ultimate symbol of a fallen king and a lost civilization.
subtitle: King of Troy, Father of Heroes
achievements: Ruled Troy during a period of prosperity
Fathered a large and influential family
Negotiated the return of Hector's body from Achilles
Maintained peace and stability within Troy for many years
Displayed courage and dignity in the face of adversity

keyMyths.title: Priam's Plea to Achilles
keyMyths.description: Priam bravely enters Achilles' camp to beg for Hector's body. He appeals to Achilles' own grief for his father, Peleus, ultimately moving the Achaean hero to compassion and allowing him to ransom his son's corpse. This act highlights Priam's courage and paternal love.

keyMyths.title: The Fall of Troy
keyMyths.description: During the sack of Troy, Priam witnesses the slaughter of his sons and the destruction of his city. He seeks refuge at the altar of Zeus Polieus, but is brutally murdered by Neoptolemus, Achilles' son, marking the end of the Trojan royal line.

keyMyths.title: Priam's Early Life
keyMyths.description: Priam, originally named Podarces, was ransomed by his sister Hesione from Heracles, who had captured and enslaved him after Laomedon, Priam's father, refused to pay him for services rendered. His name was then changed to Priam, meaning 'ransomed'.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: The primary source for Priam's role in the Trojan War, particularly his relationship with Hector and Achilles.
sources.text: The primary source for Priam's role in the Trojan War, particularly his relationship with Hector and Achilles.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Details Priam's death and the fall of Troy from the perspective of Aeneas.
sources.text: Details Priam's death and the fall of Troy from the perspective of Aeneas.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive account of Priam's lineage and early life.
